The excellent way discussed at the end of the previous chapter is not what we commonly mean by charity – almsgiving – but love in its fullest sense: true love for God and people. Without this, even the most glorious gifts are worthless to us and have no value in God’s sight. A clear mind and deep understanding are useless without a kind and charitable heart. There may be an open and generous hand where there is not a liberal and charitable heart. Doing good to others will do us no good if it is not done from love for God and goodwill toward people. If we give away everything we have while withholding our hearts from God, it will not profit us. Nor even the most painful sufferings. How deceived are those who look for acceptance and reward for their good works, which are as meager and flawed as they are selfish!

Some of the effects of charity are stated so that we may know whether we possess this grace; and if we do not, so that we will not rest until we do. This love is a clear proof of being born again, and it tests our professed faith in Christ. In this beautiful description of the nature and effects of love, the Corinthians are shown that their behavior has been contrary to it in many ways. Charity is completely opposed to selfishness; it does not desire or seek its own praise, honor, profit, or pleasure. This is not to say that charity destroys all regard for ourselves, or that a charitable person should neglect themselves and their interests. But charity never seeks its own at the expense of others, or neglects others. It always prefers the well-being of others to its private advantage. How kind and agreeable is Christian charity! How excellent Christianity would appear to the world if those who profess it were more guided by this divine principle and paid due regard to the command on which its blessed Author placed the greatest emphasis! Let us ask whether this divine love dwells in our hearts. Has this principle guided us into considerate behavior toward all people? Are we willing to put aside selfish goals and ambitions? Here is a call to watchfulness, diligence, and prayer.

Charity is far more valuable than the gifts on which the Corinthians prided themselves because it lasts longer. It is a grace that endures eternally. The present state is like childhood; the future is like adulthood. That is the difference between earth and heaven. How limited are children’s views, how confused their understanding, compared to grown men! Thus we will think of our most valued gifts in this world when we reach heaven. Everything seems dark and confusing now, compared with what it will be later. They can only be seen as by a reflection in a mirror or in the description of a riddle; but later our knowledge will be free from all obscurity and error. It is the light of heaven alone that will remove all clouds and darkness that hide God’s face from us. To summarize the excellences of charity, it is preferred not only to gifts, but also to other graces, such as faith and hope. Faith focuses on divine revelation and agrees with it, relying on the divine Redeemer. Hope looks forward to future happiness and waits for it; but in heaven, faith will be absorbed into actual sight, and hope into enjoyment. There is no room for believing and hoping when we see and enjoy. But there, love will be made perfect. There we will perfectly love God. And there we will perfectly love one another. Blessed state! How much surpassing the best below! God is love. Where God is seen as he is, face to face, there charity is at its greatest height; there only will it be perfected.
